content.full_text:
b

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

August 3, 1987

Honorable Peter Green
Councilmember, Huntington Beach
P.O. Box 190
Huntington Beach, CA  92648

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. I-87-187

Dear Councilmember Green:

     You have written seeking advice concerning your duties
under the Political Reform Act.

QUESTION

     Does the Political Reform Act permit your minor son to be
employed by the city library as a page if you have no
involvement in the hiring decision?

CONCLUSION

     You may not make, participate in making, or use your
official position to influence any decision which would
increase or decrease your son's wages by $250 or more in a
year.  We cannot provide any more detailed advice regarding
past conduct or events.

FACTS

     You are a member of the Huntington Beach City Council.
Recently, your 15-year-old son, Tom, applied for and was
selected as a library page at the Central Library.  Members of
the city council have no involvement in the selection process.
He will receive minimum wage for a part-time position of not
more than 20 hours a week.

ANALYSIS

     The Political Reform Act (the "Act")1/ provides that no
public official shall make, participate in making, or use his

1/  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise
indicated.  Commission regulations appear at 2 California
Administrative Code Section 18000, et seq.  All references to
regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California
Administrative Code.

428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Honorable Peter Green
August 3, 1987
Page 2


official position to influence the making of any governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
(Section 87100.) An official has a financial interest in a
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect on the official or a member of
the official's immediate family which is distinguishable from
the decision's effect upon the public generally. (Section
87103.)

Your 15-year-old son is a member of your immediate family.
(Section 82029.) Therefore, you must not participate in or
influence any city decision which would have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect upon him. Under the
Commission's Regulation 18702.1(a)(4), any decision which would
result in an increase or decrease in his income of $250 or more
per year would be considered material. Obviously, employment
decisions would affect him differently than the public
generally. You are required to disqualify yourself from such
decisions. A decision by another city official, acting without
your participation or input, to hire your son is permissible
since disqualification is personal as to you. We cannot
provide you with any more specific advice on this issue since
the Commission does not provide advice regarding past events or
conduct. (See Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A), copy enclosed.)

A decision to change city pay rates overall would not
require your disqualification, even though your son's wages
might be affected, because he is not being singled out for
different treatment. All city employees constitute a
significant segment of the general public for purposes of such
decisions. (See Advice Letter to Deborah Hopkins, No.
A-82-106, copy enclosed.)

Should you have questions regarding this letter, I may be
reached at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

By: Robert E. Leidigh
Counsel, Legal Division

official position to influence the making of any governmental

decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will

Should you have questions regarding this letter, I may be

Sincerely,

DMG:REL:plh

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 7, 1987

Peter Green, Councilman
City of Huntington Beach
P.O. Box 190
Huntington Beach, CA 92648

Re: 87-187

Dear Mr. Green:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform
Act was received on July 3, 1987 by the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact Robert Leidigh, an attorney in
the Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly. Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions,
or more information is needed, you should expect a response
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written
advice. If more information is needed, the person assigned to
prepare a response to your request will contact you directly to
advise you as to information needed. If your request is for
informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can.
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Adm. Code Sec. 18329).)

You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,

[signature]

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

DMG;jaj

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

City of Huntington Beach
P. O. BOX 190 • 2000 MAIN-STREET • CALIFORNIA 92648

MAYOR
Jack Kelley
MAYOR PRO TEMPORE
John Erskine
COUNCILMEMBERS
Wes Bannister
Bill Conrey
Peter Green
Tom Mays
Grace Winchell

June 29, 1987

Ms. Cathy Donovan
Fair Political Practices
Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Ms. Donovan:

My name is Peter Green, and I am a member of the Huntington Beach
City Council. Recently my 15 year old son, Tom, applied for, was
interviewed for, and received a position of Library Page at the
Central Library. Members of the City Council do not involve
themselves in the selection process. He will receive minimum wage
for a part-time position of not more than 20 hours a week.
Incidentally, he will earn no more than I do, since the city charter
sets council members salary at $186.00 a month.

I would like your opinion as to any possible violation of the
Political Reform Act in his taking this position.

Sincerely,

Peter Green
Councilman
